nodes.label: Foundations of Sensation & Perception
nodes.summary: Sensation is the detection of stimuli by sensory receptors, while perception is the brain's interpretation and organization of that sensory information.

nodes.label: Core Distinctions
nodes.summary: The basic vocabulary distinguishing how stimuli are detected versus interpreted.

nodes.label: Sensation
nodes.summary: Sensation is the process by which sensory receptors detect physical stimulus energy from the environment.

nodes.label: Perception
nodes.summary: Perception is the brain's processing, organization, and interpretation of sensory information into a meaningful experience.

nodes.label: Bottom-Up Processing
nodes.summary: Bottom-up (data-driven) processing builds perception starting from raw sensory input and assembling it into a whole.

nodes.label: Top-Down Processing
nodes.summary: Top-down (theory-driven) processing uses prior knowledge, expectations, and context to interpret sensory input.

nodes.label: Sensory Transduction
nodes.summary: How physical stimulus energy is converted into neural signals.

nodes.label: Transduction
nodes.summary: Transduction is the conversion of physical or chemical stimulus energy into an electrochemical neural signal (action potentials).

nodes.label: Sensory Receptor Types
nodes.summary: Receptors are classified by stimulus: photoreceptors (light), mechanoreceptors (pressure/movement), chemoreceptors (chemicals), thermoreceptors (temperature), and nociceptors (pain).

nodes.label: Receptor (Generator) Potential
nodes.summary: A receptor potential is a graded change in membrane potential that, if sufficient, triggers an action potential in a sensory neuron.

nodes.label: Sensory Coding
nodes.summary: Stimulus intensity is coded by action-potential frequency and number of activated receptors, while stimulus type is coded by which receptors/pathways fire (labeled lines).

nodes.label: Labeled Lines & Specific Nerve Energies
nodes.summary: The doctrine of specific nerve energies (labeled-line coding) holds that the modality perceived depends on which dedicated pathway is activated, not on the stimulus itself.

nodes.label: Receptive Field
nodes.summary: A receptive field is the region of sensory space (e.g., area of skin or retina) in which a stimulus alters a given sensory neuron's firing; smaller fields yield finer acuity.

nodes.label: Sensory Pathways & Coding
nodes.summary: The general route sensory information travels from receptor to cortex.

nodes.label: Afferent (Sensory) Neurons
nodes.summary: Afferent neurons carry sensory information from receptors toward the central nervous system.

nodes.label: First-, Second-, Third-Order Neurons
nodes.summary: Most sensory pathways relay through first-order (receptor to spinal cord/brainstem), second-order (to thalamus), and third-order (to cortex) neurons.

nodes.label: Thalamus as Sensory Relay
nodes.summary: The thalamus relays nearly all sensory information (except smell) to the appropriate primary sensory cortex.

nodes.label: Cortical Projection Areas
nodes.summary: Each sense projects to a dedicated primary cortex: visual (occipital), auditory (temporal), somatosensory (parietal), gustatory (insula/frontal), olfactory (olfactory cortex).

nodes.label: Sensory Adaptation
nodes.summary: Diminished sensitivity to constant, unchanging stimuli.

nodes.label: Sensory Adaptation
nodes.summary: Sensory adaptation is the decrease in sensitivity (receptor response) to a constant, unchanging stimulus over time.

nodes.label: Tonic vs. Phasic Receptors
nodes.summary: Tonic receptors adapt slowly and fire continuously to sustained stimuli, whereas phasic receptors adapt quickly and respond mainly to changes.

nodes.label: Sensory Adaptation vs. Habituation
nodes.summary: Sensory adaptation is a receptor-level decline in responding, whereas habituation is a central/learned decrease in behavioral response; the two are distinct mechanisms.

nodes.label: Psychophysics
nodes.summary: Psychophysics studies the relationship between the physical magnitude of stimuli and the psychological sensations they produce.

nodes.label: Thresholds
nodes.summary: The minimum stimulus levels needed for detection or discrimination.

nodes.label: Absolute Threshold
nodes.summary: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time.

nodes.label: Threshold of Conscious Perception
nodes.summary: The threshold of conscious perception is the intensity above which a stimulus reaches awareness; below it stimuli may still be processed unconsciously.

nodes.label: Difference Threshold (JND)
nodes.summary: The difference threshold, or just-noticeable difference (JND), is the minimum change in stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time.

nodes.label: Subliminal Stimuli
nodes.summary: Subliminal stimuli fall below the absolute threshold of conscious perception but may still be detected and weakly influence processing.

nodes.label: Psychophysical Laws
nodes.summary: Quantitative relations between stimulus magnitude and perceived intensity.

nodes.label: Weber's Law
nodes.summary: Weber's law states the JND is a constant proportion (Weber fraction, ΔI/I = k) of the original stimulus intensity, not a fixed amount.

nodes.label: Fechner's Law
nodes.summary: Fechner's law states perceived sensation magnitude grows logarithmically with stimulus intensity (S = k log I).

nodes.label: Stevens' Power Law
nodes.summary: Stevens' power law states perceived magnitude is a power function of stimulus intensity, fitting some sensations better than Fechner's log relation.

nodes.label: Signal Detection Theory
nodes.summary: How detection decisions reflect both sensitivity and non-sensory factors like expectation and motivation.

nodes.label: Signal Detection Theory (SDT)
nodes.summary: Signal detection theory explains how internal (psychological/contextual) and external factors influence whether a stimulus is detected against background noise.

nodes.label: Hits, Misses, False Alarms, Correct Rejections
nodes.summary: SDT outcomes are hits, misses, false alarms, and correct rejections, arranged by whether a signal was present and whether the observer responded.

nodes.label: Response Bias (Criterion)
nodes.summary: Response bias is the observer's decision criterion, shifted by motivation/expectation, separate from true sensory sensitivity (d').

nodes.label: Sensitivity (d')
nodes.summary: Sensitivity (d-prime) quantifies how well an observer can discriminate signal from noise, independent of their response criterion/bias.

nodes.label: Noise
nodes.summary: Noise is irrelevant internal or external activity against which a true signal must be detected.

nodes.label: Vision
nodes.summary: The visual system transduces light into neural signals processed for color, form, motion, and depth.

nodes.label: Eye Anatomy & Light Path
nodes.summary: The structures that focus light onto the retina.

nodes.label: Cornea
nodes.summary: The cornea is the transparent outer layer that performs most of the eye's light refraction.

nodes.label: Aqueous Humor
nodes.summary: The aqueous humor is the watery fluid in the anterior chamber that nourishes the cornea and lens and maintains intraocular pressure.

nodes.label: Iris & Pupil
nodes.summary: The iris is the pigmented muscle that controls pupil size, regulating the amount of light entering the eye.

nodes.label: Lens & Accommodation
nodes.summary: The lens changes shape via the ciliary muscles (accommodation) to focus images at varying distances onto the retina.

nodes.label: Vitreous Humor
nodes.summary: The vitreous humor is the gel filling the posterior eye that maintains its shape and transmits light to the retina.

nodes.label: Sclera
nodes.summary: The sclera is the tough white outer layer that protects and maintains the shape of the eyeball.

nodes.label: Choroid
nodes.summary: The choroid is the vascular middle layer that supplies blood to the retina and absorbs stray light.

nodes.label: Refractive Errors (Myopia, Hyperopia, Astigmatism)
nodes.summary: Myopia (nearsightedness) focuses images in front of the retina, hyperopia (farsightedness) behind it, and astigmatism reflects an irregularly curved cornea/lens.

nodes.label: Presbyopia & Near/Far Point
nodes.summary: Presbyopia is age-related loss of accommodation as the lens stiffens, raising the near point (closest distance of clear focus).

nodes.label: Visual Acuity
nodes.summary: Visual acuity is the sharpness or resolving power of vision, highest at the fovea and dependent on proper refraction onto the retina.

nodes.label: Retina & Photoreceptors
nodes.summary: The neural layer that transduces light into signals.

nodes.label: Retinal Cell Layers
nodes.summary: Light passes through ganglion and bipolar cell layers to reach photoreceptors at the back, with signals relayed back forward to ganglion cells.

nodes.label: Rods
nodes.summary: Rods are highly light-sensitive photoreceptors for dim-light (scotopic) and peripheral vision, but do not detect color.

nodes.label: Cones
nodes.summary: Cones are photoreceptors for color and fine-detail (photopic) vision, concentrated in the fovea and active in bright light.

nodes.label: Duplexity (Duplicity) Theory
nodes.summary: Duplexity theory holds that the retina contains two separate systems—rods for low-light/achromatic vision and cones for bright-light/color vision.

nodes.label: Three Cone Types (S, M, L)
nodes.summary: Three cone types respond maximally to short (blue), medium (green), and long (red) wavelengths, underlying trichromatic color vision.

nodes.label: Fovea
nodes.summary: The fovea is the cone-dense central retinal pit that provides the sharpest visual acuity.

nodes.label: Bipolar & Ganglion Cells
nodes.summary: Bipolar cells relay photoreceptor signals to ganglion cells, whose axons form the optic nerve.

nodes.label: Horizontal & Amacrine Cells
nodes.summary: Horizontal and amacrine cells provide lateral connections that enable lateral inhibition and edge enhancement in the retina.

nodes.label: Optic Disc (Blind Spot)
nodes.summary: The optic disc is where the optic nerve exits the retina; it lacks photoreceptors, creating the blind spot.

nodes.label: Phototransduction
nodes.summary: The molecular conversion of light into a neural signal.

nodes.label: Photopigments (Rhodopsin)
nodes.summary: Photopigments such as rhodopsin contain retinal bound to opsin; light isomerizes retinal, initiating phototransduction.

nodes.label: Photoreceptor Hyperpolarization
nodes.summary: Light causes photoreceptors to hyperpolarize and reduce glutamate release, an unusual response to a stimulus.

nodes.label: Dark & Light Adaptation
nodes.summary: Dark adaptation regenerates photopigment for dim-light sensitivity, while light adaptation rapidly reduces sensitivity in bright light.

nodes.label: Visual Pathways
nodes.summary: How visual signals travel from retina to cortex.

nodes.label: Optic Nerve & Optic Chiasm
nodes.summary: Ganglion axons form the optic nerve; at the optic chiasm, nasal-retina fibers cross so each hemisphere processes the contralateral visual field.

nodes.label: Lateral Geniculate Nucleus
nodes.summary: The lateral geniculate nucleus of the thalamus relays visual information to the primary visual cortex.

nodes.label: Primary Visual Cortex (V1)
nodes.summary: The primary visual cortex in the occipital lobe receives and begins cortical processing of visual input.

nodes.label: Feature Detectors
nodes.summary: Feature detectors are cortical neurons (e.g., Hubel and Wiesel's) that respond to specific stimulus features like edges, orientation, or movement.

nodes.label: Parallel Processing
nodes.summary: Parallel processing simultaneously analyzes separate features of a scene (color, motion, form, depth) along distinct pathways.

nodes.label: Ventral (What) & Dorsal (Where) Streams
nodes.summary: The ventral 'what' stream (to temporal lobe) processes object identity, while the dorsal 'where/how' stream (to parietal lobe) processes spatial location and motion.

nodes.label: Color Vision Theories
nodes.summary: Complementary theories explaining how color is perceived.

nodes.label: Trichromatic (Young-Helmholtz) Theory
nodes.summary: The trichromatic theory holds that color vision arises from combined activity of three cone types tuned to red, green, and blue.

nodes.label: Opponent-Process Theory
nodes.summary: Opponent-process theory holds that color is encoded by opposing pairs (red-green, blue-yellow, black-white), explaining afterimages.

nodes.label: Color Blindness
nodes.summary: Color blindness, often X-linked, results from missing or defective cone types, most commonly red-green deficiency.

nodes.label: Afterimages
nodes.summary: Negative afterimages occur when opponent-process channels fatigue, producing the complementary color after staring at a stimulus.

nodes.label: Hearing (Audition)
nodes.summary: The auditory system transduces sound-wave vibrations into neural signals encoding pitch, loudness, and location.

nodes.label: Sound Wave Properties
nodes.summary: The physical characteristics of sound that map to auditory experience.

nodes.label: Frequency & Pitch
nodes.summary: Sound-wave frequency (Hz) determines perceived pitch; humans hear roughly 20–20,000 Hz.

nodes.label: Amplitude & Loudness
nodes.summary: Sound-wave amplitude (measured in decibels) determines perceived loudness.

nodes.label: Timbre
nodes.summary: Timbre is the perceived quality of a sound arising from its mixture of frequencies, distinguishing sources of the same pitch.

nodes.label: Outer & Middle Ear
nodes.summary: Structures that collect and amplify sound.

nodes.label: Pinna & Auditory Canal
nodes.summary: The pinna (auricle) gathers sound and funnels it through the auditory canal toward the eardrum.

nodes.label: Tympanic Membrane (Eardrum)
nodes.summary: The tympanic membrane vibrates in response to sound waves, transferring vibrations to the ossicles.

nodes.label: Ossicles (Malleus, Incus, Stapes)
nodes.summary: The three ossicles—malleus, incus, and stapes—amplify and transmit eardrum vibrations to the oval window.

nodes.label: Oval Window
nodes.summary: The stapes presses on the oval window, transmitting vibrations into the fluid-filled cochlea.

nodes.label: Inner Ear & Transduction
nodes.summary: Where mechanical vibration becomes a neural signal.

nodes.label: Cochlea
nodes.summary: The cochlea is the fluid-filled, coiled inner-ear structure that houses the organ of hearing.

nodes.label: Basilar Membrane
nodes.summary: The basilar membrane vibrates within the cochlea, with different regions resonating to different frequencies (tonotopic organization).

nodes.label: Organ of Corti & Hair Cells
nodes.summary: The organ of Corti sits on the basilar membrane and contains hair cells that transduce vibrations into neural signals.

nodes.label: Hair Cell Stereocilia Bending
nodes.summary: Bending of hair-cell stereocilia opens ion channels, depolarizing the cell and triggering auditory nerve signals.

nodes.label: Tectorial Membrane
nodes.summary: The tectorial membrane overlies the hair cells; relative motion bends their stereocilia during sound transduction.

nodes.label: Round Window
nodes.summary: The round window is a flexible membrane that bulges to relieve fluid pressure as the oval window drives the incompressible cochlear fluid.

nodes.label: Auditory Pathway & Pitch Theories
nodes.summary: How auditory signals reach cortex and how pitch is encoded.

nodes.label: Auditory Nerve to Cortex
nodes.summary: The vestibulocochlear nerve carries auditory signals via the brainstem and medial geniculate nucleus to the auditory cortex in the temporal lobe.

nodes.label: Place Theory
nodes.summary: Place theory holds that pitch is determined by which location on the basilar membrane vibrates most, best for high frequencies.

nodes.label: Frequency (Temporal) Theory
nodes.summary: Frequency theory holds that pitch is encoded by the rate of neural firing matching the sound's frequency, best for low frequencies.

nodes.label: Volley Principle
nodes.summary: The volley principle extends frequency theory: groups of neurons fire in alternating volleys to encode frequencies above a single neuron's firing limit.

nodes.label: Tonotopic Organization
nodes.summary: Tonotopy is the orderly frequency-to-place mapping preserved from the basilar membrane through to the auditory cortex.

nodes.label: Sound Localization
nodes.summary: Sound is localized using interaural time and intensity differences between the two ears.

nodes.label: Conductive vs. Sensorineural Hearing Loss
nodes.summary: Conductive loss involves outer/middle-ear transmission failure, while sensorineural loss involves cochlear hair-cell or auditory-nerve damage.

nodes.label: Vestibular Sense (Equilibrioception)
nodes.summary: The vestibular system in the inner ear detects head position, balance, and acceleration.

nodes.label: Vestibular Structures
nodes.summary: Inner-ear organs that sense rotation and linear movement.

nodes.label: Semicircular Canals
nodes.summary: The three semicircular canals detect rotational (angular) acceleration of the head in three planes.

nodes.label: Otolithic Organs (Utricle & Saccule)
nodes.summary: The utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration and head position relative to gravity via otoliths.

nodes.label: Vestibular Hair Cells
nodes.summary: Hair cells in vestibular organs transduce fluid (endolymph) movement into neural signals about head motion.

nodes.label: Balance & Proprioceptive Integration
nodes.summary: How vestibular input supports equilibrium.

nodes.label: Equilibrium & Balance
nodes.summary: The vestibular system integrates with vision and proprioception to maintain balance and a stable sense of body orientation.

nodes.label: Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex
nodes.summary: The vestibulo-ocular reflex stabilizes gaze by moving the eyes opposite to head movement.

nodes.label: Motion Sickness & Sensory Conflict
nodes.summary: Motion sickness arises from sensory-conflict (mismatch) between vestibular and visual cues about self-motion.

nodes.label: Somatosensation
nodes.summary: Somatosensation encompasses touch, pressure, temperature, pain, and body position from skin and internal receptors.

nodes.label: Touch & Pressure Receptors
nodes.summary: Mechanoreceptors in the skin that detect physical contact.

nodes.label: Cutaneous Mechanoreceptors
nodes.summary: Mechanoreceptors such as Merkel cells, Meissner's, Pacinian, and Ruffini corpuscles detect touch, pressure, vibration, and stretch.

nodes.label: Pacinian Corpuscles
nodes.summary: Pacinian corpuscles are deep, rapidly-adapting receptors that detect deep pressure and high-frequency vibration.

nodes.label: Two-Point Discrimination
nodes.summary: Two-point discrimination measures tactile acuity, which is finest where receptor density and cortical representation are greatest (e.g., fingertips, lips).

nodes.label: Temperature & Pain
nodes.summary: Thermoreceptors and nociceptors detecting temperature and tissue damage.

nodes.label: Thermoreceptors
nodes.summary: Thermoreceptors detect warmth and cold, responding most to changes in skin temperature.

nodes.label: Free Nerve Endings
nodes.summary: Free nerve endings are unencapsulated afferent terminals in the skin that serve as the primary receptors for temperature and pain.

nodes.label: Nociceptors
nodes.summary: Nociceptors are free nerve endings that detect noxious stimuli (mechanical, thermal, chemical) and signal pain.

nodes.label: Gate Control Theory of Pain
nodes.summary: Gate control theory proposes that a spinal 'gate' can block or allow pain signals, modulated by non-painful input and top-down brain signals.

nodes.label: Referred & Phantom Pain
nodes.summary: Referred pain is perceived at a site distinct from its source due to shared pathways, while phantom limb pain is felt in a missing limb, illustrating central pain processing.

nodes.label: Proprioception & Kinesthesia
nodes.summary: Sensing body position and movement.

nodes.label: Proprioception
nodes.summary: Proprioception is the sense of the relative position and movement of body parts, via receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints.

nodes.label: Kinesthesia
nodes.summary: Kinesthesia is the sense of body movement and limb motion, closely tied to proprioception.

nodes.label: Muscle Spindles & Golgi Tendon Organs
nodes.summary: Muscle spindles detect muscle stretch and Golgi tendon organs detect muscle tension, providing proprioceptive feedback.

nodes.label: Somatosensory Cortex
nodes.summary: Cortical mapping of body sensation.

nodes.label: Sensory Homunculus
nodes.summary: The somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus, parietal lobe) maps the body, with area proportional to sensitivity rather than size (homunculus).

nodes.label: Chemical Senses (Taste & Smell)
nodes.summary: Gustation and olfaction use chemoreceptors to detect dissolved or airborne chemicals.

nodes.label: Taste (Gustation)
nodes.summary: Detection of chemicals dissolved in saliva.

nodes.label: Taste Buds & Receptor Cells
nodes.summary: Taste buds on the tongue's papillae contain gustatory receptor cells that transduce dissolved chemicals.

nodes.label: Five Basic Tastes
nodes.summary: The five basic tastes are sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami (savory).

nodes.label: Gustatory Pathway
nodes.summary: Taste signals travel via cranial nerves to the brainstem and thalamus, reaching the gustatory cortex (insula/frontal operculum).

nodes.label: Taster Variation (Supertasters)
nodes.summary: Individuals vary in taste sensitivity; supertasters have high papillae/receptor density and heightened sensitivity to bitter compounds (e.g., PTC/PROP).

nodes.label: Smell (Olfaction)
nodes.summary: Detection of airborne chemical molecules.

nodes.label: Olfactory Receptor Neurons
nodes.summary: Olfactory receptor neurons in the nasal epithelium bind odorant molecules and transduce them into neural signals.

nodes.label: Olfactory Bulb & Direct Cortical Route
nodes.summary: Olfactory signals project via the olfactory bulb directly to the cortex and limbic system, bypassing the thalamus.

nodes.label: Pheromones
nodes.summary: Pheromones are chemical signals released by one organism that can influence the behavior or physiology of another.

nodes.label: Olfaction's Link to Memory & Emotion
nodes.summary: Olfaction's direct limbic connections give smell a strong, immediate link to memory and emotion.

nodes.label: Flavor (Taste-Smell Integration)
nodes.summary: Flavor is the multimodal percept produced by combining gustation, olfaction (often retronasal), and texture/temperature into a unified experience.

nodes.label: Perceptual Organization & Constancies
nodes.summary: The brain organizes raw sensory input into coherent, stable percepts of form, depth, motion, and constancy.

nodes.label: Gestalt Principles
nodes.summary: Rules by which the brain groups elements into wholes.

nodes.label: Gestalt Principles (Overview)
nodes.summary: Gestalt principles describe how the brain organizes parts into a whole, where the whole is perceived as more than the sum of its parts.

nodes.label: Proximity
nodes.summary: Proximity is the Gestalt principle that elements close together are perceived as a group.

nodes.label: Similarity
nodes.summary: Similarity is the Gestalt principle that similar elements are grouped together.

nodes.label: Continuity (Good Continuation)
nodes.summary: Continuity is the Gestalt principle that we perceive smooth, continuous patterns rather than abrupt breaks.

nodes.label: Closure
nodes.summary: Closure is the Gestalt principle that we fill in gaps to perceive a complete, whole object.

nodes.label: Common Fate
nodes.summary: Common fate is the Gestalt principle that elements moving together in the same direction are perceived as a single group.

nodes.label: Symmetry
nodes.summary: Symmetry (prägnanz/good form) is the Gestalt tendency to perceive figures as simple, orderly, and symmetrical.

nodes.label: Figure-Ground
nodes.summary: Figure-ground organization is the perceptual separation of an object (figure) from its background (ground).

nodes.label: Law of Prägnanz
nodes.summary: The law of Prägnanz holds that we perceive ambiguous stimuli in the simplest, most stable form possible.

nodes.label: Depth Perception
nodes.summary: Cues that yield three-dimensional perception from two-dimensional retinal input.

nodes.label: Binocular Cues
nodes.summary: Binocular depth cues use both eyes: retinal disparity (differing images) and convergence (inward eye rotation for near objects).

nodes.label: Retinal (Binocular) Disparity
nodes.summary: Retinal disparity is the difference between the two eyes' images, which the brain uses to compute depth.

nodes.label: Convergence
nodes.summary: Convergence is the inward turning of the eyes for nearby objects, providing a depth cue.

nodes.label: Monocular Cues
nodes.summary: Monocular cues (one eye) include relative size, interposition, linear perspective, texture gradient, relative height, and motion parallax.

nodes.label: Perceptual Constancy
nodes.summary: Perceiving objects as stable despite changing sensory input.

nodes.label: Perceptual Constancy (Overview)
nodes.summary: Perceptual constancy is perceiving objects as unchanging in size, shape, color, and lightness despite changes in retinal images.

nodes.label: Size Constancy
nodes.summary: Size constancy is perceiving an object as the same size regardless of changes in its retinal image with distance.

nodes.label: Shape, Color & Lightness Constancy
nodes.summary: Shape, color, and lightness constancies preserve perceived form and appearance despite changes in viewing angle or illumination.

nodes.label: Motion Perception & Illusions
nodes.summary: Perception of movement and systematic perceptual errors.

nodes.label: Motion Perception
nodes.summary: Motion perception detects movement from changes across the retina and is supported by dedicated motion-sensitive pathways.

nodes.label: Phi Phenomenon & Apparent Motion
nodes.summary: The phi phenomenon is illusory (apparent) motion perceived when stationary lights flash in sequence, as in motion pictures.

nodes.label: Perceptual Illusions
nodes.summary: Perceptual illusions (e.g., Müller-Lyer, moon illusion, Ponzo) reveal the brain's reliance on cues and assumptions that can misfire.

nodes.label: Top-Down Influences on Perception
nodes.summary: How expectations and context shape what we perceive.

nodes.label: Perceptual Set
nodes.summary: A perceptual set is a mental predisposition to perceive one thing and not another, shaped by experience and expectation.

nodes.label: Context Effects
nodes.summary: Context effects describe how the surrounding environment influences perception of a stimulus.

nodes.label: Schemata & Prior Experience
nodes.summary: Schemata are mental frameworks from experience that guide top-down interpretation of sensory input.

nodes.label: Culture, Emotion & Motivation Effects
nodes.summary: Cultural background, emotional state, and motivation bias perception, illustrating that top-down factors extend beyond simple expectation.

nodes.label: Extrasensory Perception (ESP) & Parapsychology
nodes.summary: ESP is the claimed perception of stimuli without sensory input (telepathy, clairvoyance, precognition); parapsychology studies it but it lacks reproducible scientific support.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Cerebral cortex lobes and localization of function
Lenses, focal length, and image formation
Corrective lenses, diverging vs. converging lenses
Wavelength and energy of electromagnetic (visible) light
Sound waves, frequency, and wave properties
Muscle structure and the stretch reflex
